text: The surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath on the patient's left side near the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal space behind the diastatic linea alba. The procedure progresses towards the cephalad direction, revealing scarring of the rectus muscle against the posterior sheath. The surgeon attempts to identify the linea alba while separating the rectus muscle from the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon is in the intraperitoneal position, working to take down adhesions or existing peritoneum towards the xiphoid. The posterior rectus sheath is being cut at the cephalad portion of the chevron incision in the midline. The entry into the right retrorectus space above the chevron incision is visible. The surgeon uses a grasper to identify and hug the linea alba, approaching the main incisional hernia. The hernia sac is reduced, revealing the medial edge of the larger defect. The omentum is taken down from the hernia sac, completing the pseudosac takedown on the medial side of the defect.

The surgeon navigates the abdominal cavity to visualize the hernia contents at the right lateral corner of the chevron scar. The retrorectus position is maintained despite minimal rectus muscle presence at the linea semilunaris. Dissection of the retrorectus space is performed lateral to the chevron scar. The surgeon initiates the transversus abdominis release at the xiphoid area, identifying a natural tissue plane in the fatty layer and dissecting behind the diaphragm before cutting the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon carefully avoids bringing down diaphragm fibers while dissecting under the diaphragm for about four to five centimeters. The top-down transversus abdominis release (TAR) is continued, starting with an incision in the posterior lamella of the internal oblique. The surgeon uses one side of the scissor to shimmy behind the muscle, lifting it, and employs cautery and scissors to cut the transversus abdominis. The transition from the diaphragm to the transversus abdominis is visible, with the surgeon staying close to the muscle to maintain a pre-transversalis plane in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon navigates through a thick layer of transversalis fascia, avoiding perforation of the peritoneum. Despite scarring and limited posterior sheath near the linea semilunaris, a plane is identified between the transverse abdominis and transversalis fascia. The transition from the pre-transversalis plane to the pre-peritoneal plane is visible, followed by the takedown of the peritoneum. The caudal dissection of the TAR is completed, revealing an extensive pocket for optimal mesh overlap.

The surgeon measures the hernia defect, which spans nine centimeters in the cranial-caudal direction and nineteen centimeters transversely. Multiple V-lock sutures are laid out across the defect without tightening, resembling a corset when eventually tightened to distribute tension evenly. The defect encompasses the diastatic linea alba but does not extend to the left side. The posterior layer is closed to prevent bowel entry into the retro-rectus space, avoiding re-approximation of the posterior rectus sheath due to excessive tension.

The surgeon closes the available material, including peritoneum, posterior sheath, or pseudosac, to patch large defects. A 30 by 30 centimeter piece of medium weight macroporous polypropylene mesh is cut and rounded at the corners, providing a good overlap of the defect. A 19 French round JP drain is placed due to the large space. The mesh appears slightly redundant with some wrinkling and overlap.
